Could I get...?+ nounCould I get the vegetables?
DicaUse instead of para trocar (vegetables instead of potatoes). I'll have = I will have. Adicione please.

Stage 13.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a palavra correta. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

"Could I see the , please?"
"To start, I would like the soup as an ."
"For the main course, I will the salmon."
"Could I get the vegetables the potatoes?"
"I have a mild to nuts."
"Could we have the , please?"

Stage 2: Put the Order in Order

Order

Coloque as etapas de um pedido em restaurante na ordem correta.

?For dessert, I will have the fruit tart.
?To start, I would like the soup.
?Could we have the bill, please?
?For the main course, I will have the salmon.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesmo dizendo-a.

To start, I would like the tomato soup.
Para começar, eu gostaria da sopa de tomate.
For the main course, I will have the salmon.
Para o prato principal, vou querer o salmão.
Could I get the vegetables instead of the potatoes?
Eu poderia trocar para os legumes no lugar das batatas?
I have a mild allergy to nuts.
Tenho uma leve alergia a castanhas.
Could we have the bill, please?
Poderíamos pegar a conta, por favor?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada momento no restaurante.

The server is ready to take your order. You start with:
A "Give me soup."
B "To start, I would like the tomato soup."
C "Soup now."
You order the main course. You say:
A "I will have the grilled salmon."
B "I am having salmon yesterday."
C "Salmon, fast."
You want to change a side dish. You say:
A "No potatoes, vegetables."
B "Could I get the vegetables instead of the potatoes?"
C "Change my food."
You finished your meal and want to pay. You say:
A "Could we have the bill, please?"
B "Money now."
C "I pay where?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesmo respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Imagine you are at a restaurant in Washington after the congress. Order a full meal: an appetizer, a main course (with one change, "instead of"), a dessert, and a beverage. Mention an allergy, and at the end, ask for the bill. Use I would like... / I'll have... / Could I get...? Speak for one to two minutes.

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1To start, I would like the tomato soup.Para começar, eu gostaria da sopa de tomate.
2For the main course, I will have the salmon.Para o prato principal, vou querer o salmão.
3Could I get the vegetables instead of the potatoes?Eu poderia trocar para os legumes no lugar das batatas?
4I have a mild allergy to nuts.Tenho uma leve alergia a castanhas.
5Could we have the bill, please?Poderíamos pegar a conta, por favor?
Aula 14 -- Pre-class

Getting Around Washington

Asking for and following directions in the city. Key words: corner, block, sidewalk, crosswalk, avenue, traffic light, landmark, subway station, intersection, across from. Structures: imperatives for directions (Go straight / Turn left / Take / Cross) and How do I get to...? / Where is...? / Is it far?
0%
▼

Stage 14.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. Toque em Listen para escutar.

Corner -- esquina (where two streets meet)
"The bank is on the corner of K Street."
Block -- quarteirão (the area between two streets)
"Go straight for two blocks."
Sidewalk -- calçada (the path beside the road)
"Stay on the sidewalk and keep walking."
Crosswalk -- faixa de pedestres (where you cross safely)
"Cross the road at the crosswalk."
Avenue -- avenida (a wide, important street)
"Walk down this avenue for three blocks."
Traffic light -- semáforo (the signal that controls cars)
"At the second traffic light, turn left."
Landmark -- ponto de referência (a famous building to find your way)
"The museum is a good landmark to follow."
Subway station -- estação de metrô (where you take the underground train)
"The hotel is next to the subway station."
Intersection -- cruzamento (where two roads cross)
"Turn right at the next intersection."
Across from -- em frente a (on the opposite side of)
"The hotel is across from the park."

Stage 14.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

Corner
Block
Sidewalk
Crosswalk
Avenue
Traffic light
Landmark
Subway station
Intersection
Across from

Stage 14.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

After dinner, Marcos leaves the restaurant and asks a local for directions. "Excuse me, how do I get to the conference center? Is it far?" The woman answers using the imperative: "Go straight for two blocks. At the second traffic light, turn left. Walk past the coffee shop. The building is on the corner, across from the park, next to the subway station." Notice the directions: each one starts with a base verb (Go, Turn, Walk, Cross), with no subject and no "to". Marcos repeats the steps to confirm and says: "Thank you so much."

1. Que pergunta Marcos usa para pedir o caminho?
A "Where you go?"
B "How do I get to the conference center? Is it far?"
C "Conference center please."
2. Como começam as direções no texto?
A Com "You" + verbo.
B Com o verbo base (Go, Turn, Walk), sem sujeito.
C Com "To" + verbo.
3. Which is the correct imperative for directions?
A "You turn to left."
B "Turn left at the traffic light."
C "To turning left."

Stage 14.4: Grammar Tip -- Imperatives for Directions

GRAMMAR

Como dar direções com o imperativo (explicação em inglês e português).

VerbForm / FormaExample
Goverbo base, sem sujeitoGo straight for two blocks.
Turn+ left / right (sem "to")Turn left at the light.
Take / Cross+ o objetoCross the road at the crosswalk.
DicaImperativo = só o verbo base. Sem "I", sem "you", sem "to". Para perguntar: How do I get to...? / Where is...? / Is it far?

Stage 14.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a palavra correta. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

" straight for two blocks."
"At the second traffic light, left."
" the road at the crosswalk."
"The hotel is the park."
"How do I the subway station?"
"The bank is on the ."

Stage 2: Put the Directions in Order

Order

Coloque as etapas das direções na ordem correta.

?Walk past the coffee shop and the bank.
?Go straight for two blocks.
?The building is on the corner, across from the park.
?At the second traffic light, turn left.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesmo dizendo-a.

Excuse me, how do I get to the conference center?
Com licença, como eu chego ao centro de convenções?
Go straight for two blocks.
Vá em frente por dois quarteirões.
Turn left at the traffic light.
Vire à esquerda no semáforo.
Cross the road at the crosswalk.
Atravesse a rua na faixa de pedestres.
The hotel is across from the park.
O hotel fica em frente ao parque.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada momento na rua.

You are lost and want to ask the way. You say:
A "Where conference center?"
B "Excuse me, how do I get to the conference center?"
C "You take me there."
You give directions. You say:
A "Go straight and turn left at the traffic light."
B "You going straight to left."
C "To go straight left."
You want to describe where the bank is. You say:
A "Bank near here."
B "The bank is on the corner, across from the park."
C "Bank in front park."
You want to know the distance. You ask:
A "Is it far from here?"
B "How much distance?"
C "It is long way?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesmo respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Imagine you are in Washington and someone asks you the way from your hotel to the conference center. Give full directions using imperatives (Go straight, Turn left, Walk past, Cross) and prepositions of place (on the corner, across from, next to). Then explain how far it is. Speak for one to two minutes.

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1Excuse me, how do I get to the conference center?Com licença, como eu chego ao centro de convenções?
2Go straight for two blocks.Vá em frente por dois quarteirões.
3Turn left at the traffic light.Vire à esquerda no semáforo.
4It is across from the park, next to the subway station.Fica em frente ao parque, ao lado da estação de metrô.
5Is it far from here?É longe daqui?
Aula 15 -- Pre-class

Building Relationships -- Social Dinners & Invitations

Inviting, accepting and declining at a social dinner. Key words: invitation, invite, accept, decline, available, reschedule, gathering, get-together, treat, spare. Structures: making invitations (Would you like to...? / Why don't we...? / How about + -ing? / Are you free on...?), accepting (I'd love to / That sounds great) and declining politely (I'm afraid I can't / Maybe another time).
0%
▼

Stage 15.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. Toque em Listen para escutar.

Invitation -- convite (a request to come to an event)
"Thank you for the dinner invitation."
Invite -- convidar (to ask someone to come or join you)
"Let me invite you to dinner tonight."
Accept -- aceitar (to say yes to an invitation)
"I am happy to accept your invitation."
Decline -- recusar (to politely say no)
"I had to decline politely because I was busy."
Available -- disponível (free and able at a certain time)
"Are you available for dinner on Friday?"
Reschedule -- remarcar (to change a plan to another time)
"Can we reschedule the dinner for next week?"
Gathering -- encontro/reunião (a meeting for a social event)
"We are having a small gathering after the panel."
Get-together -- encontro informal (a casual, informal meeting)
"It is just a relaxed get-together after work."
Treat -- pagar para alguém (to pay for someone as a kind gesture)
"Please, order anything. Dinner is my treat tonight."
Spare -- de sobra/livre (extra and free, not being used)
"Do you have a spare hour for a quick dinner?"

Stage 15.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

Invitation
Invite
Accept
Decline
Available
Reschedule
Gathering
Get-together
Treat
Spare

Stage 15.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

After the sessions, Marcos meets a counterpart and makes an invitation: "Would you like to join us for dinner tonight? How about meeting at seven thirty?" She is happy to accept: "I'd love to. That sounds great." Notice the forms: after Would you like we use to + verb (to join), and after How about we use the -ing form (meeting). Later, another colleague has to decline politely: "Thank you, but I'm afraid I can't tonight. Maybe another time." Marcos says it is no problem and offers to reschedule: "Are you available on Friday?" This way, every invitation keeps the relationship warm, whether the answer is yes or no.

1. Que forma vem depois de "Would you like"?
A O verbo no -ing (joining).
B "to" + verbo base (to join).
C O verbo sozinho, sem "to" (join).
2. Que forma vem depois de "How about"?
A O verbo no -ing (meeting).
B "to" + verbo (to meet).
C "will" + verbo (will meet).
3. Which is a polite way to decline an invitation?
A "No, I don't want."
B "Thank you, but I'm afraid I can't. Maybe another time."
C "I am can't go."

Stage 15.4: Grammar Tip -- Making, Accepting & Declining Invitations

GRAMMAR

Como convidar, aceitar e recusar (explicação em inglês e português).

FunctionForm / FormaExample
InviteWould you like to + verbo / How about + -ingWould you like to join us? / How about meeting?
Acceptaceitar com entusiasmoI'd love to. / That sounds great.
Declinerecusar com educaçãoI'm afraid I can't. Maybe another time.
DicaApós Would you like = to + verbo. Após How about / Why don't we = -ing / verbo base. Para recusar: I'm afraid I can't (nunca "I am can't").

Stage 15.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a palavra correta. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

"Would you like join us for dinner?"
"How about at seven thirty?"
"Are you on Friday evening?"
"I'd love to. Thank you for the ."
"I'm afraid I can't tonight. Can we for next week?"
"Please, order anything. Dinner is my tonight."

Stage 2: Put the Conversation in Order

Order

Coloque as falas do convite na ordem correta.

?How about meeting at seven thirty?
?Would you like to join us for dinner tonight?
?Perfect, see you there. Thank you for the invitation.
?I'd love to. What time were you thinking?

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesmo dizendo-a.

Would you like to join us for dinner tonight?
Você gostaria de jantar conosco hoje à noite?
How about meeting at seven thirty?
Que tal nos encontrarmos às sete e meia?
I'd love to. That sounds great.
Eu adoraria. Parece ótimo.
I'm afraid I can't tonight, but maybe another time.
Infelizmente não posso hoje, mas quem sabe outra hora.
Are you available for dinner on Friday?
Você está disponível para jantar na sexta-feira?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada momento social.

You want to invite a counterpart to dinner. You say:
A "You come dinner now."
B "Would you like to join us for dinner tonight?"
C "Dinner you want?"
A colleague invites you and you want to say yes. You say:
A "I'd love to. That sounds great."
B "Yes I am agree."
C "Okay maybe yes."
You are busy and must say no kindly. You say:
A "No. I am can't."
B "Thank you, but I'm afraid I can't. Maybe another time."
C "I no want, sorry."
You want to suggest a different day. You say:
A "Can we reschedule for Friday? Are you available then?"
B "We change day Friday yes?"
C "Friday is possible to you?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesmo respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Imagine you are at the Washington congress and you want to invite a counterpart to a social dinner. Make the invitation (Would you like to...? / How about...? / Are you free on...?), suggest a time and a place, and respond to both a "yes" and a "no". Then practice declining an invitation politely, with an alternative. Speak for one to two minutes.

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1Would you like to join us for dinner tonight?Você gostaria de jantar conosco hoje à noite?
2How about meeting at seven thirty?Que tal nos encontrarmos às sete e meia?
3I'd love to. That sounds great.Eu adoraria. Parece ótimo.
4I'm afraid I can't tonight, but maybe another time.Infelizmente não posso hoje, mas quem sabe outra hora.
5Thank you for the invitation.Obrigado pelo convite.
Aula 16 -- Pre-class

Talking About Goals & the Future

Talking about goals, plans and predictions. Key words: goal, ambition, aim, objective, vision, pursue, aspire, long-term, candidate, seek. Grammar: future forms -- "will" (predictions, decisions, promises) vs "going to" (plans, intentions) + time expressions (next year, in five years, by 2030, soon).
0%
▼

Stage 16.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. Toque em Listen para escutar.

Goal -- meta/objetivo (something you want to achieve)
"My main goal is to lead the association."
Ambition -- ambição (a strong desire to achieve something)
"His ambition is to become president of the board."
Aim -- propósito/alvo (the purpose you want to reach)
"Our aim is to grow the team this year."
Objective -- objetivo (a clear, specific result you want)
"The main objective is to expand into Europe."
Vision -- visão (a clear idea of the future you want)
"I have a clear vision for the next five years."
Pursue -- buscar/perseguir (to work hard to reach a goal)
"I want to pursue the presidency of the association."
Aspire -- aspirar (to strongly hope to reach something high)
"I aspire to lead an international organization."
Long-term -- de longo prazo (lasting far into the future)
"My long-term goal is the presidency."
Candidate -- candidato (a person who runs for a position)
"I am going to run as a candidate for the board."
Seek -- procurar/buscar (to try to find or get something)
"I seek a leadership role in the next few years."

Stage 16.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

Goal
Ambition
Aim
Objective
Vision
Pursue
Aspire
Long-term
Candidate
Seek

Stage 16.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

At the dinner, a counterpart asks Marcos about his goals. Marcos shares his plan: "This year, I am going to finish my certification. Next year, I am going to lead two projects." Notice that for plans he already decided, he uses going to. Then he makes a prediction: "I think I will be ready to run as a candidate by then." For predictions and beliefs, we use will. His long-term vision is clear: "In five years, I am going to pursue the presidency." When the mentor offers help, Marcos answers on the spot: "Thank you, I will send you my proposal today." A decision made in the moment also uses will. Remember: will + base verb (never "will to"), and going to + base verb for plans.

1. Que forma usamos para um plano que já decidimos?
A "will" + verbo (will lead).
B "going to" + verbo (going to lead).
C "will to" + verbo (will to lead).
2. Que forma usamos para uma previsão ou crença?
A "will" + verbo base (I think I will win).
B "going to" sempre.
C presente simples (I win).
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "I will to pursue the presidency."
B "I am going to pursue the presidency."
C "I am going pursue the presidency."

Stage 16.4: Grammar Tip -- will vs going to

GRAMMAR

Como falar do futuro (explicação em inglês e português).

Form / FormaUse / UsoExample
going to + verboplano / intenção já decidida (decided plan)I am going to lead two projects.
will + verboprevisão / crença (prediction)I think I will win.
will + verbodecisão na hora / promessa (on the spot)I will send it today.
Dicawill + verbo base (NUNCA "will to"). going to + verbo base para planos. Expressões: next year, in five years, by 2030, soon.

Stage 16.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a palavra correta. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

"Next year, I am to lead two projects."
"I think I win the election."
"My long-term is to lead the association."
"In five years, I want to the presidency."
"I am going to run as a for the board."
"I to lead an international organization."

Stage 2: Put the Conversation in Order

Order

Coloque a conversa sobre o futuro na ordem correta.

?This year, I am going to finish my certification.
?Where do you see yourself in five years?
?In five years, I will pursue the presidency.
?My long-term goal is to lead the association.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesmo dizendo-a.

Next year, I am going to lead two projects.
Ano que vem, eu vou liderar dois projetos.
I think I will win the election.
Eu acho que vou vencer a eleição.
My long-term goal is to lead the association.
Minha meta de longo prazo é liderar a associação.
In five years, I am going to pursue the presidency.
Em cinco anos, vou buscar a presidência.
I aspire to be a leader in my field.
Eu aspiro a ser um líder na minha área.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada momento.

You describe a plan you already decided for next year. You say:
A "Next year I will to lead a project."
B "Next year I am going to lead a project."
C "Next year I going lead a project."
You make a prediction about your future. You say:
A "I think I will become a leader in the field."
B "I think I will to become a leader."
C "I think I become leader."
A colleague needs help and you decide on the spot. You say:
A "I am going to help you." (already planned)
B "Don't worry, I will help you."
C "I will to help you."
You describe your long-term ambition. You say:
A "My long-term goal is to pursue the presidency."
B "My goal long-term to president."
C "I will to be president long-term."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesmo respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Imagine a counterpart at a congress asks about your future. Describe your five-year plan: use "going to" for plans you already decided and "will" for predictions. Mention your goals, your ambition, and a time expression (next year, in five years). Speak for one to two minutes.

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1Next year, I am going to lead two projects.Ano que vem, vou liderar dois projetos.
2I think I will succeed in the long term.Eu acho que vou ter sucesso no longo prazo.
3My long-term goal is to lead the association.Minha meta de longo prazo é liderar a associação.
4In five years, I am going to pursue the presidency.Em cinco anos, vou buscar a presidência.
5I aspire to be a leader in my field.Eu aspiro a ser um líder na minha área.
Aula 17 -- Pre-class

Negotiating & Building Partnerships

Negotiating a partnership: making proposals, bargaining terms and reaching agreement. Key words: negotiate, agreement, deal, terms, offer, compromise, concession, bargain, commitment, leverage. Grammar: first conditional for negotiation -- if + present, will + verb ("If you lower the price, we will sign today") + agreement language.
0%
▼

Stage 17.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. Toque em Listen para escutar.

Negotiate -- negociar (to discuss terms to reach an agreement)
"We need to negotiate the terms of the partnership."
Agreement -- acordo (an arrangement two sides accept)
"After two hours, we reached an agreement."
Deal -- negócio/acordo (a business arrangement both sides accept)
"I think we have a fair deal for both sides."
Terms -- termos/condições (the conditions of an agreement)
"We are flexible on the terms of the deal."
Offer -- oferta (something you are willing to give or do)
"Here is our offer: full access to the network."
Compromise -- meio-termo (an agreement where both sides give a little)
"We found a good compromise on the price."
Concession -- concessão (something you give up so the other side agrees)
"Both sides made a few concessions today."
Bargain -- barganhar/pechinchar (to discuss a price to get a better deal)
"They bargained over the fee for an hour."
Commitment -- compromisso (a serious promise to do something)
"We are ready to make a strong commitment."
Leverage -- vantagem/poder de barganha (an advantage that gives more power)
"This partnership gives us real leverage in the market."

Stage 17.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

Negotiate
Agreement
Deal
Terms
Offer
Compromise
Concession
Bargain
Commitment
Leverage

Stage 17.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

Marcos sits down to negotiate a partnership with a firm from Canada. The counterpart makes an offer: "If you join our network this year, we will share our cross-border deals." Notice the pattern: after if we use the present (you join), and in the result we use will + verb (we will share). Marcos replies with his own condition: "If your team includes a training program, we will sign." He bargains on the terms: "I am afraid the fee is too high. Can we meet in the middle?" The other side makes a concession: "If you sign this week, we will cut the fee in half." They reach a good compromise and shake hands: "We have a deal." Remember the golden rule: NEVER use will after if -- the if-clause uses the present.

1. Depois de "if", qual forma do verbo usamos?
A will + verbo (If you will join).
B presente simples (If you join).
C going to (If you are going to join).
2. No resultado (a outra parte da frase), o que usamos?
A will + verbo base (we will share).
B presente simples (we share).
C if + presente de novo.
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "If you will lower the price, we will sign."
B "If you lower the price, we will sign."
C "If you lower the price, we sign."

Stage 17.4: Grammar Tip -- First Conditional

GRAMMAR

Como negociar com condições (explicação em inglês e português).

Part / ParteForm / FormaExample
if-clause (condição)if + presente simplesIf you lower the price...
resultado (result)will + verbo base...we will sign today.
Ordem flexívelWe will agree if you include training. (sem vírgula quando "if" vem no meio)
Regra de ouroNUNCA use "will" depois de "if". Acordo: What if...? / We could offer... / That works for us / I'm afraid that won't work / Can we meet in the middle? / We have a deal.

Stage 17.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a palavra correta. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

"If you the price, we will sign today."
"If you include the training, we agree."
"I think we have a fair for both sides."
"We are flexible on the of the deal."
"Both sides made a few today."
"This partnership gives us real in the market."

Stage 2: Put the Conversation in Order

Order

Coloque a negociação na ordem correta.

?If you sign this week, we will cut the fee in half.
?Here is our offer: full access to the network.
?That is a fair compromise. We have a deal.
?I'm afraid the fee is too high. Can we meet in the middle?

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesmo dizendo-a.

If you lower the price, we will sign today.
Se você baixar o preço, nós assinamos hoje.
What if we include a training program?
E se nós incluíssemos um programa de treinamento?
I'm afraid that won't work. Can we meet in the middle?
Receio que isso não dê certo. Podemos chegar a um meio-termo?
That is a fair compromise. We have a deal.
É um meio-termo justo. Temos um acordo.
This partnership gives us real leverage.
Essa parceria nos dá uma vantagem real.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada momento.

You want to offer to sign if they lower the price. You say:
A "If you will lower the price, we will sign."
B "If you lower the price, we will sign."
C "If you lower the price, we sign."
You want to propose adding something to the deal. You say:
A "What if we also include a training program?"
B "We will to include a training program."
C "If we will include a program."
The fee is too high and you push back politely. You say:
A "No. The fee is bad."
B "I'm afraid that won't work. Can we meet in the middle?"
C "If you will cut the fee, we agree."
You accept the final offer. You say:
A "That works for us. We have a deal."
B "We will have deal if you will sign."
C "Yes deal for me."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesmo respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Imagine you are negotiating a partnership with a foreign firm. Make an offer using the first conditional ("If you..., we will..."), then push back on one term and propose a compromise. Use words like deal, terms, offer, concession and compromise. Speak for one to two minutes.

Survival Card -- Lesson 17

1If you lower the price, we will sign today.Se você baixar o preço, nós assinamos hoje.
2What if we include a training program?E se incluíssemos um programa de treinamento?
3I'm afraid that won't work. Can we meet in the middle?Receio que não dê certo. Podemos chegar a um meio-termo?
4That is a fair compromise. We have a deal.É um meio-termo justo. Temos um acordo.
5This partnership gives us real leverage.Essa parceria nos dá uma vantagem real.
Aula 18 -- Pre-class

The Confident Communicator (Review & Integration)

Capstone review of the whole Washington congress arc (lessons 11-17). Key review words: boarding pass, reservation, menu, server, bill, landmark, crosswalk, invitation, goal, negotiate. Structures review: polite requests (Could I have...?), imperatives for directions, invitations (Would you like...?), future (will / going to) and the first conditional + agreement language -- all in one integrated simulation.
0%
▼

Stage 18.1: Vocabulary Review

Vocabulary

Revise as palavras-chave da viagem. Toque em Listen para escutar.

Boarding pass -- cartão de embarque (the card that lets you board a plane)
"I showed my boarding pass at the gate."
Reservation -- reserva (a room or table booked in advance)
"I confirmed my reservation at the front desk."
Menu -- cardápio (the list of food and drinks)
"Could I see the menu, please?"
Server -- garçom/garçonete (the person who brings your food)
"I asked the server for a glass of water."
Bill -- conta (the paper that shows how much you pay)
"Could we have the bill, please?"
Landmark -- ponto de referência (a famous place to find your way)
"The hotel is near a famous landmark."
Crosswalk -- faixa de pedestres (where you cross the street)
"Turn left at the crosswalk."
Invitation -- convite (when someone asks you to an event)
"I received an invitation to the dinner."
Goal -- meta/objetivo (something you want to achieve)
"One of my goals is to grow internationally."
Negotiate -- negociar (to discuss terms to reach a deal)
"I negotiated a small partnership at the congress."

Stage 18.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

Boarding pass
Reservation
Menu
Server
Bill
Landmark
Crosswalk
Invitation
Goal
Negotiate

Stage 18.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

A confident communicator uses the right structure at the right moment. At the airport, Marcos showed his boarding pass. At the hotel, he said: "Could I have the key, please?" -- a polite request. At the restaurant he asked the server: "Could we have the menu and, later, the bill?" To find the congress, he asked for directions and heard imperatives: "Go straight and turn left at the crosswalk." That evening he got an invitation: "Would you like to join us?" He spoke about his goals: "Next year, I am going to grow internationally." Finally, he closed a deal: "If you send the terms, we will sign." Remember the golden rule: NEVER use will after if -- the if-clause uses the present.

1. Para pedir algo educadamente (o menu, a conta), qual estrutura usamos?
A imperativo (Give me the menu).
B pedido polido (Could I have the menu?).
C condicional (If I have the menu).
2. Para dar direções, qual forma do verbo usamos?
A imperativo / verbo base (Go straight, turn left).
B will + verbo (You will go straight).
C Could + verbo (Could you go straight?).
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "If you will send the terms, we will sign."
B "If you send the terms, we will sign."
C "If you send the terms, we sign."

Stage 18.4: Grammar Tip -- Structures Map

GRAMMAR

Um mapa das estruturas do arco -- qual usar em cada momento (inglês e português).

Momento / MomentEstrutura / StructureExample
pedir/pedir comida (asking)Could I have...? / I would like...Could I have the bill?
direções (directions)imperativo (verbo base)Go straight, turn left.
convidar (inviting)Would you like...? / How about...?Would you like to join us?
planos futuros (future)will / going to + verboI am going to grow.
fechar o acordo (the deal)if + presente, will + verboIf you send it, we will sign. (NUNCA "will" depois de "if")

Stage 18.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a palavra correta. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

" I have the bill, please?"
"Go straight and left at the crosswalk."
"Would you to join us for dinner?"
"Next year, I am to grow internationally."
"If you send the terms, we sign."
"I received an to the networking dinner."

Stage 2: Put the Day in Order

Order

Coloque o dia do congresso na ordem correta.

?Go straight and turn left at the crosswalk to the congress.
?I showed my boarding pass at the airport.
?If you send the terms, we will sign. We have a deal.
?At the restaurant, could I have the menu, please?

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesmo dizendo-a.

Could I have the menu, please?
Posso ver o cardápio, por favor?
Go straight and turn left at the crosswalk.
Siga reto e vire à esquerda na faixa de pedestres.
Would you like to join us for dinner?
Você gostaria de jantar conosco?
Next year, I am going to grow internationally.
No ano que vem, eu vou crescer internacionalmente.
If you send the terms, we will sign.
Se você enviar os termos, nós assinamos.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada momento da viagem.

At the restaurant, you want the menu. You say:
A "Give me the menu."
B "Could I have the menu, please?"
C "If I have the menu, I will order."
Someone asks the way to the congress. You say:
A "Go straight and turn left at the crosswalk."
B "You will go to straight and turn to left."
C "Would you go straight at the crosswalk?"
You want to invite a colleague to dinner. You say:
A "You come to dinner."
B "Would you like to join us for dinner?"
C "If you will join, we have dinner."
You want to close the partnership with a condition. You say:
A "If you send the terms, we will sign."
B "If you will send the terms, we sign."
C "You send terms, we signing."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesmo respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Tell the story of a full business trip abroad, from start to finish. Use a polite request (Could I have...?), a direction (go straight, turn left), an invitation (Would you like...?), a future goal (I am going to...) and a conditional deal (If you..., we will...). Use words like reservation, menu, bill, landmark and invitation. Speak for one to two minutes, with confidence.

Survival Card -- Lesson 18

1Could I have the menu, please?Posso ver o cardápio, por favor?
2Go straight and turn left at the crosswalk.Siga reto e vire à esquerda na faixa de pedestres.
3Would you like to join us for dinner?Você gostaria de jantar conosco?
4Next year, I am going to grow internationally.No ano que vem, eu vou crescer internacionalmente.
5If you send the terms, we will sign.Se você enviar os termos, nós assinamos.
Aula 19 -- Pre-class

Professional Emails & Following Up

Write professional follow-up emails after the Washington congress: attach files, reply, forward and reach out. New grammar: the present perfect (have/has + past participle) with for, since, yet, already and just -- "I have sent the report", "Have you received my email?", "I haven't heard back yet".
0%
▼

Stage 19.1: Vocabulary

Vocabulary

Aprenda as palavras do e-mail profissional. Toque em Listen para escutar.

Attach -- anexar (to add a file to an email)
"Please attach the report to your reply."
Attachment -- anexo (the file you send with an email)
"I could not open the attachment on my phone."
Draft -- rascunho (a first version, not final yet)
"I have written a draft of the contract."
Forward -- encaminhar (to send a received email on to someone)
"Could you forward that message to my team?"
Reply -- responder / resposta (to answer an email)
"I will reply to your message by Friday."
Recipient -- destinatário (the person who receives the email)
"Check the recipient before you press send."
Subject line -- linha de assunto (the title line of an email)
"Write a clear subject line so people open it."
Reach out -- entrar em contato (to contact someone, often new)
"I will reach out to the people I met in Washington."
Sincerely -- atenciosamente (a formal way to close an email)
"Sincerely, Marcos Mansour."
Acknowledge -- confirmar o recebimento (to confirm you received something)
"Please acknowledge this email when it arrives."
Pending -- pendente (still waiting, not finished yet)
"Two replies are still pending from last week."
Enclosed -- incluso / em anexo (included with a letter or email)
"Please find the signed contract enclosed."

Stage 19.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

Attach
Draft
Forward
Reply
Recipient
Subject line
Acknowledge
Pending

Stage 19.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

Back from the congress, Marcos opens his inbox. "I have sent the proposal," he tells his partner, "but I haven't heard back yet." A new contact, Rachel, has just replied. She asks: "Have you received my email?" Marcos answers: "Yes, I have already opened the attachment, thank you." He has reached out to three people since Monday, and two replies are still pending. We use the present perfect (have/has + past participle) for actions that connect to NOW -- with yet, already, just, for and since. But for a finished time (like "yesterday"), we use the past simple: "I sent it yesterday."

1. Para uma ação ligada ao AGORA (com yet/already/just), qual tempo usamos?
A past simple (I sent).
B present perfect (I have sent).
C present continuous (I am sending).
2. Como se forma o present perfect?
A have/has + past participle (have sent).
B have/has + verbo base (have send).
C will + verbo (will send).
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "I have sent it yesterday."
B "I sent it yesterday."
C "I sending it yesterday."

Stage 19.4: Grammar Tip -- The Present Perfect

GRAMMAR

A estrutura do present perfect, com afirmativa, negativa e pergunta (inglês e português).

Forma / FormEstrutura / StructureExample
afirmativa (affirmative)have / has + past participleI have sent it. / She has replied.
negativa (negative)have not (haven't) / has not (hasn't) + past participleI haven't heard back yet.
pergunta (question)Have / Has + sujeito + past participleHave you received it?
marcadores (time words)for, since, yet, already, justI have worked here for five years. (NUNCA com "yesterday")

Stage 19.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

"I have the report."
" you received my email?"
"She has replied to my message."
"I haven't opened the attachment ."
"We have signed the contract."
"Please the file to your reply."

Stage 2: Put the Email in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de um follow-up por e-mail na ordem correta.

?I haven't heard back yet, so I will send a follow-up.
?I have written a clear subject line for my email.
?She has just replied and acknowledged my message.
?I have attached the report and sent it to the recipient.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesmo dizendo-a.

I have attached the report to this email.
Anexei o relatório a este e-mail.
Have you received my email?
Você recebeu meu e-mail?
I am still waiting for your reply.
Ainda estou esperando sua resposta.
Could you forward me the attachment?
Você poderia me encaminhar o anexo?
I have already acknowledged your email.
Já confirmei o recebimento do seu e-mail.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação de e-mail.

You finished the report and sent it. You write:
A "I have send the report."
B "I have sent the report."
C "I have sent the report yesterday."
You want to ask a client if your email arrived. You say:
A "Have you received my email?"
B "You have received my email?"
C "Did you have received my email?"
You are still waiting for a reply. You say:
A "I didn't hear back yet."
B "I haven't heard back yet."
C "I don't hear back yet."
You can't find the file. You ask:
A "Could you forward me the attachment again?"
B "Could you forward me the recipient again?"
C "Could you reply me the subject line again?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesmo respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Imagine you are following up with a contact you met at a congress. In one to two minutes, say what you have already done (sent, attached, forwarded) and what is still pending. Use the present perfect (I have sent..., Have you received...?, I haven't heard back yet) and email words like attachment, reply, draft and reach out.

Survival Card -- Lesson 19

1I have attached the report to this email.Anexei o relatório a este e-mail.
2Have you received my email?Você recebeu meu e-mail?
3I haven't heard back yet.Ainda não tive retorno.
4Could you forward me the attachment?Você poderia me encaminhar o anexo?
5I will reply by Friday. Sincerely, Marcos.Responderei até sexta. Atenciosamente, Marcos.
Aula 20 -- Pre-class

On a Call -- Telephone & Video Conferences

Handle phone calls and lead video conferences with your international contacts: answer professionally, take and pass on messages, and fix tech issues. New grammar: reported speech (say / tell / ask if) -- "He said he would call back", "She told me the meeting was moved", "He asked if I was available".
0%
▼

Stage 20.1: Vocabulary

Vocabulary

Aprenda as palavras do telefone e da videoconferência. Toque em Listen para escutar.

Dial -- discar (to enter a phone number to call)
"Please dial the international code first."
Caller -- quem liga (the person making the call)
"The caller did not leave a name."
Extension -- ramal (an internal number for a person at a company)
"You can reach me on extension 204."
Voicemail -- correio de voz (a recorded message you leave)
"I left a voicemail, but he hasn't called back."
Hang up -- desligar (to end a phone call)
"Don't hang up -- I'll transfer you now."
On hold -- na espera (waiting on a paused phone line)
"They put me on hold for ten minutes."
Get through -- conseguir falar / completar a ligação (to reach someone by phone)
"I tried all morning, but I couldn't get through."
Mute -- mudo (to turn your microphone off)
"You're on mute -- we can't hear you."
Share screen -- compartilhar a tela (to show your screen on a video call)
"Let me share my screen so you can see the figures."
Breaking up -- falhando / cortando (when the connection cuts in and out)
"Sorry, you're breaking up -- could you repeat that?"
Background noise -- ruído de fundo (other sounds during a call)
"There's a lot of background noise -- let me move to a quiet room."
Speak up -- falar mais alto (to talk more loudly)
"Could you speak up a little? You're very quiet."

Stage 20.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

Dial
Caller
Extension
Voicemail
Hang up
On hold
Mute
Share screen

Stage 20.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

After the call, Marcos passes on the message to his partner. "Rachel called," he says. "She told me she was happy with the proposal, and she said she would send a meeting link this afternoon. David asked if we could move our call to Friday, because he said he was stuck in another meeting." We use reported speech to tell someone what another person said. We use say with no person and tell with a person (told me). For questions, we use ask if / whether. The verb usually steps back one tense: is → was, will → would, can → could.

1. Qual verbo usamos COM uma pessoa (told me)?
A say (say me).
B tell (tell me / told me).
C ask (ask me that).
2. "I will call" reported becomes:
A He said he would call.
B He said he will call.
C He said he calls.
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "He asked if I am free."
B "He asked if I was free."
C "He asked am I free."

Stage 20.4: Grammar Tip -- Reported Speech

GRAMMAR

Como reportar o que alguém disse, com say, tell e ask if (inglês e português).

Forma / FormEstrutura / StructureExample
say (sem pessoa / no person)said (that) + fraseHe said he would call back.
tell (com pessoa / + person)told + pessoa + (that) + fraseShe told me the meeting was on Friday.
pergunta (question)asked + if / whether + fraseHe asked if I was available.
backshift (um passo atrás)is→was, will→would, can→could, have→had"I can hear you" → She said she could hear me.

Stage 20.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

"He said he call back."
"She me the meeting was on Friday."
"He asked I was available."
"They said they sent the report."
"She said she hear me."
"Sorry, you're up."

Stage 2: Put the Call in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de uma ligação profissional na ordem correta.

?He said he couldn't join, so I offered to take a message.
?I answered the phone: "Marcos speaking."
?I passed the message on: "He asked if we could meet on Friday."
?The caller asked to speak to my colleague.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesmo dizendo-a.

Marcos speaking. How can I help?
Marcos falando. Como posso ajudar?
Can I take a message?
Posso anotar um recado?
He said he would call you back.
Ele disse que ligaria de volta.
Sorry, you're breaking up. Can you hear me?
Desculpe, você está cortando. Você me ouve?
Could you share your screen, please?
Você poderia compartilhar a tela, por favor?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação de chamada.

You answer a work call. You say:
A "Who are you?"
B "Marcos speaking. How can I help?"
C "What do you want?"
You want to report David's words: "I will be late." You say:
A "He said me he will be late."
B "He said he would be late."
C "He told he will be late."
A colleague's microphone is off. You say:
A "You're on mute -- we can't hear you."
B "You're on hold -- we can't hear you."
C "You hang up -- we can't hear you."
You want to report a question: "Are you free on Friday?" You say:
A "He asked if I was free on Friday."
B "He asked if I am free on Friday."
C "He asked am I free on Friday."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesmo respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Imagine you are passing on a phone message to a colleague. In one to two minutes, report what two different people said and asked, using reported speech (He said he would..., She told me she was..., He asked if I could...) and call words like voicemail, breaking up, share screen and on hold.

Survival Card -- Lesson 20

1Marcos speaking. How can I help?Marcos falando. Como posso ajudar?
2Can I take a message?Posso anotar um recado?
3He said he would call you back.Ele disse que ligaria de volta.
4Sorry, you're breaking up. Can you hear me?Desculpe, você está cortando. Você me ouve?
5Could you share your screen, please?Você poderia compartilhar a tela, por favor?

IN CLASS -- Selecione a Aula

01
Introducing Yourself Professionally
Meeting a colleague at an international congress -- 28 slides
02
Your Career Journey
Telling the story of your professional path -- 29 slides
03
Your Field — Valuation & Appraisal
Explaining what you do to a non-expert -- 29 slides
04
Small Talk & Networking at Events
Starting and keeping a conversation going -- 29 slides
05
Introducing & Presenting Other People
Presenting one person to another -- 29 slides
06
Asking Questions in Sessions (Q&A)
Polite indirect questions in a session -- 29 slides
07
Answering Questions About Your Work
Clarifying, paraphrasing, buying time -- 29 slides
08
Giving a Short Professional Presentation
Structuring a talk with signposting -- 29 slides
09
Trends -- AI in Valuation
Describing trends with present continuous -- 29 slides
10
Meetings -- the UPAV Committee
Meeting language and making suggestions -- 29 slides
11
At the Airport -- Immigration
Travel questions and answers -- 29 slides
12
Hotel Check-in & Getting Help
Polite requests with can and could -- 29 slides
13
Eating Out & Ordering
Ordering with would like and I'll have -- 29 slides
14
Getting Around Washington
Asking for and following directions with imperatives -- 29 slides
15
Building Relationships
Social dinners and invitations: inviting, accepting and declining -- 29 slides
16
Talking About Goals
Goals and the future: plans, predictions and ambitions (will / going to) -- 29 slides
17
Negotiating Partnerships
Negotiating and building partnerships: proposals, terms and reaching agreement (first conditional + agreement language) -- 29 slides
18
The Confident Communicator
Capstone review: the full Washington congress experience integrated -- requests, ordering, directions, invitations, future plans, conditionals and agreement (review of lessons 11-17) -- 29 slides
19
Professional Emails & Following Up
Write professional follow-up emails after the congress -- attachments, replies and staying in touch -- with the present perfect (have/has + past participle; for/since/yet/already/just) -- 29 slides
20
On a Call -- Telephone & Video Conferences
Handle phone calls and lead video conferences with international contacts -- answering, taking messages and fixing tech issues -- with reported speech (He said he would call back; She told me the meeting was moved; He asked if I was available) -- 29 slides

Atividades Complementares

Materiais extras para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula. Marque como concluído após assistir/ouvir.

Series
Suits -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

Professionals at a top New York law firm constantly introduce themselves, state their roles, and exchange contacts. Observe how they say what they do, name their company, and make a strong first impression. Direct connection to Lesson 1: professional self-introductions and networking in a high-stakes environment.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Note 3 expressions the characters use to introduce themselves and their roles.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
The English We Speak (BBC) -- "Introducing Yourself and Others"

Short, clear episodes about everyday professional English. Listen to natural phrases used when meeting people, stating your role, and keeping in touch. Direct connection to Lesson 1: the real expressions native speakers use for self-introductions and small talk.

Tip: listen twice. Write down 5 phrases you can use to introduce yourself at the congress.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
"How to Introduce Yourself Professionally in English" -- Business English Pod

A practical lesson on professional self-introductions: name, role, company, and field, plus the questions you ask back. Connection to Lesson 1: ready-to-use sentence patterns for networking events and congresses, exactly the context Marcos will face in Washington.

Tip: pause and repeat each introduction aloud. Practice saying your own version 3 times.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
The Founder (2016 film)

The story of how a man built a global company step by step. Listen to how the characters describe their past decisions, first jobs, and turning points, all in the past simple. Direct connection to Lesson 2: narrating a career journey and the milestones that shaped it.

Tip: note 5 past-tense verbs (regular and irregular) the characters use to describe how the business grew.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
How I Built This (NPR) -- any founder episode

Founders tell the story of how they started and grew their companies. Every episode is a career journey in the past simple: where they studied, their first jobs, their setbacks, and their milestones. Direct connection to Lesson 2: real narratives of professional growth.

Tip: pick one founder. Write a 5-step timeline of their career using time markers (then, after that, later).

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
"Talk About Your Career & Work Experience in English" -- Business English Pod

A practical lesson on describing your professional background: education, first jobs, promotions, and achievements, all using the past simple. Connection to Lesson 2: ready-to-use sentence patterns for telling your career story at a congress, exactly what Marcos will do in Washington.

Tip: pause and repeat each example aloud. Then say your own version using your real dates.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
Grand Designs (Channel 4)

In each episode, experts inspect properties and discuss what they are worth, in clear English. Listen to how they describe a building, its condition, and its value to a general audience. Direct connection to Lesson 3: explaining property and value in simple words.

Tip: note how the experts explain value WITHOUT heavy jargon. Write 5 plain-English phrases they use.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Planet Money (NPR) -- episodes on how things are priced

Planet Money explains complex economic and pricing ideas in simple, everyday English. Many episodes describe step-by-step how the value of something is decided. Direct connection to Lesson 3: turning a technical process into a clear, jargon-free explanation.

Tip: pick one episode. Summarize the process in 4 steps using first, then, after that, finally.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
"How to Explain Your Job in English (Describing a Process)" -- Business English Pod

A practical lesson on describing what you do and how a process works, step by step, using sequencing words and present simple. Connection to Lesson 3: the exact language Marcos needs to explain valuation to non-experts at the congress.

Tip: pause and repeat each step aloud. Then describe your own four-step process from memory.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
Suits (USA Network) -- networking scenes

The characters constantly work a room, break the ice, and turn small talk into business. Watch how they open a conversation, find common ground, and keep it flowing. Direct connection to Lesson 4: starting and sustaining a professional conversation.

Tip: note 5 open questions characters use to keep people talking. Try them in your next call.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
How to Win Friends and Influence People (audiobook/podcast summaries)

Classic, practical advice on starting conversations, showing genuine interest, and remembering names -- all in clear English. Direct connection to Lesson 4: the mindset and language of natural small talk and networking.

Tip: pick one chapter. Write 3 open questions you can use to show interest in someone new.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
"English Small Talk: How to Make Conversation" -- Business English Pod

A practical lesson on opening conversations, safe topics, open questions, and connectives like "by the way." Connection to Lesson 4: the exact phrases Marcos needs to keep networking conversations alive at the congress.

Tip: pause and repeat each opener aloud. Then improvise a one-minute small talk from memory.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
The Office (US) -- introduction scenes

Characters constantly introduce each other and describe what colleagues do. Watch how they use "This is..." and describe roles in the third person. Direct connection to Lesson 5: presenting one person to another and the third person -s.

Tip: note 5 introductions. Write each one as "This is X. He/She + verb + s."

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
How I Built This (NPR) -- guest introductions

At the start of each episode, the host introduces the guest and describes what they do, using clear third-person English. Direct connection to Lesson 5: presenting a person and describing their role to an audience.

Tip: listen to the first two minutes. Copy how the host introduces the guest, then introduce a colleague the same way.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
"Introducing People in English (Formal & Informal)" -- Business English Pod

A practical lesson on "This is...", "Have you met...?", and replying politely with "It is a pleasure". Connection to Lesson 5: the exact phrases Marcos needs to connect people at the congress.

Tip: pause and repeat each introduction. Then introduce two imaginary colleagues to each other from memory.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
TED Talks -- the Q&A after the talk

Many TED events include a short interview or audience Q&A. Watch how people ask polite, indirect questions and how speakers clarify their points. Direct connection to Lesson 6: asking and answering questions in a session.

Tip: note 5 polite question openers ("Could you tell me...", "I was wondering..."). Reuse them.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Freakonomics Radio -- interview episodes

The host asks experts careful, indirect questions and often asks them to clarify or explain briefly. Direct connection to Lesson 6: how to frame a polite question and ask for clarification.

Tip: pick one episode. Write down 3 questions the host asks, then rewrite a direct version of each.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
"Indirect Questions in English (Polite Questions)" -- Business English Pod

A practical lesson on "Could you tell me...?", "I was wondering...", and the word order in indirect questions. Connection to Lesson 6: the exact forms Marcos needs to ask polite questions in a session.

Tip: pause and repeat. Then turn 5 direct questions into indirect ones from memory.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
Shark Tank -- founders answering tough questions

Founders face hard, rapid questions from investors and must answer calmly. Watch how the best ones buy time, paraphrase, and stay honest under pressure. Direct connection to Lesson 7: answering tough questions about your work.

Tip: note 5 time-buying openers founders use ("That is a great question..."). Reuse them.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
The Tim Ferriss Show -- expert interviews

Guests are asked complex questions and often paraphrase to make their answers clear ("in other words...", "to put it simply..."). Direct connection to Lesson 7: clarifying and buying time when answering.

Tip: pick one episode. Note 3 times a guest paraphrases an answer; copy the phrase they use.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
"How to Answer Difficult Questions in English (Buying Time)" -- Business English Pod

A practical lesson on time-buying openers, paraphrasing, and staying honest when you do not know. Connection to Lesson 7: the exact phrases Marcos needs to answer tough questions calmly.

Tip: pause and repeat each phrase. Then answer 3 imaginary hard questions using the answer frame from memory.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
Apple & Google keynote presentations

Product keynotes are masterclasses in signposting: a clear opening, an agenda, smooth transitions ("now let us move on to..."), and a strong close. Direct connection to Lesson 8: structuring a professional presentation.

Tip: note 5 signposting phrases the presenter uses. Mark where they open, transition, and close.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
TED Talks Daily -- well-structured talks

Every TED talk is a short, structured presentation with a clear overview, ordered points, and a memorable key takeaway. Direct connection to Lesson 8: building a talk that opens, develops, and closes with signposting.

Tip: pick one talk. Write its outline in 4 parts (overview, points, transition, takeaway).

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
"Presentation Signposting Language in English" -- Business English Pod

A practical lesson on the exact phrases for opening, ordering, transitioning, and closing a talk. Connection to Lesson 8: the signposting Marcos needs to give a clear, professional presentation.

Tip: pause and repeat each signpost. Then give a one-minute talk about your work using all four stages from memory.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
The Age of A.I. (YouTube Originals series)

A documentary series on how artificial intelligence is changing industries right now. Direct connection to Lesson 9: it shows real trends and uses the present continuous constantly ("AI is transforming...", "companies are adopting...").

Tip: write down 5 sentences in the present continuous about a change you hear ("X is changing...").

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
a16z Podcast -- episodes on AI and industry

Investors and engineers describe emerging trends and how firms are adopting new tools. Direct connection to Lesson 9: trend vocabulary (emerging, data-driven, automate) and present continuous in real business speech.

Tip: note 3 new words for trends. Then describe a trend in your own field using them.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
"Present Continuous for Trends and Changes" -- English with business examples

A grammar lesson on using is/are + -ing to describe changes happening now. Connection to Lesson 9: the exact structure Marcos needs to talk about trends in valuation.

Tip: pause and build your own sentence after each example ("In my industry, ... is ...ing").

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
The West Wing -- staff and cabinet meetings

A classic series full of meetings: agendas, suggestions, and decisions made around a table. Direct connection to Lesson 10: meeting language and the polite way to suggest, propose, and put things to a vote.

Tip: note 5 phrases people use to suggest or propose ideas in a meeting.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Meeting English" episodes

A series of episodes on running and joining meetings in English: opening, suggesting, agreeing, and voting. Direct connection to Lesson 10: the exact phrases Marcos needs for the UPAV committee.

Tip: write down 3 ways to make a suggestion (Let's..., Why don't we..., How about...).

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
"Useful English Phrases for Running a Meeting" -- business English lesson

A practical lesson on chairing a meeting: opening, inviting comments, making suggestions, and taking a vote. Connection to Lesson 10: leading and contributing to a committee meeting with confidence.

Tip: practice chairing a short meeting aloud: open it, invite an idea, and put it to a vote.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
Border Security / Airport documentaries

Real footage of immigration and customs at airports, with officers asking travelers the standard questions. Direct connection to Lesson 11: the exact Q&A you will hear at passport control in Washington.

Tip: note 5 questions the officers ask. Practice a short, clear answer for each.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Travel English -- "At the Airport" episodes

Practical episodes on airport and immigration English: checking in, going through security, and answering an officer's questions. Direct connection to Lesson 11: travel vocabulary and Q&A.

Tip: write down 3 questions and your own answers for your Washington trip.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
"US Immigration Interview -- What They Ask & How to Answer"

A walkthrough of a typical US immigration interview, with the questions and good answers. Connection to Lesson 11: exactly what Marcos needs for landing at Washington Dulles.

Tip: pause after each question and answer aloud before the video does.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
The White Lotus -- hotel guest interactions

A series set in luxury hotels, full of check-in scenes, requests, and complaints to the front desk. Direct connection to Lesson 12: the polite (and not-so-polite) ways guests ask for things and report problems.

Tip: note 5 requests guests make. Rewrite any rude ones as polite "Could you...?" requests.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Travel English -- "At the Hotel" episodes

Practical episodes on checking in, asking for amenities, and solving problems politely. Direct connection to Lesson 12: hotel vocabulary and polite requests with can and could.

Tip: write 3 polite requests you might make at your Washington hotel.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
"Hotel Check-in English -- Polite Requests with Could"

A practical lesson on checking in and making polite requests with can, could, and would it be possible. Connection to Lesson 12: exactly the language Marcos needs at his Washington hotel.

Tip: pause after each model request and say your own version aloud.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
The Bear -- restaurant and kitchen English

A series set inside a restaurant, full of menus, orders, and service language. Direct connection to Lesson 13: how dishes are described and how guests order at the table.

Tip: note 5 dish names and 3 ways guests order ("I'll have...", "Could I get...").

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Travel English -- "At the Restaurant" episodes

Practical episodes on ordering food, asking for recommendations, mentioning allergies, and asking for the bill. Direct connection to Lesson 13: restaurant vocabulary and ordering phrases.

Tip: write down your own order for a 3-course meal using "I would like / I'll have".

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
"Restaurant English -- Ordering Food with Would Like and I'll Have"

A practical lesson on ordering at a restaurant, asking for recommendations, and dealing with allergies. Connection to Lesson 13: exactly the language Marcos needs for dinner in Washington.

Tip: pause after each model order and say your own version aloud.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
House of Cards -- Washington DC on screen

A series set in Washington DC, full of city streets, avenues, and landmarks. Direct connection to Lesson 14: notice how characters move around the city and name the places (the Capitol, K Street, the subway).

Tip: note 5 places in the city and 3 prepositions of place ("across from", "next to", "on the corner").

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Travel English -- "Asking for Directions" episodes

Practical episodes on asking the way, following directions, and describing locations. Direct connection to Lesson 14: imperatives (Go straight, Turn left) and the question How do I get to...?

Tip: write directions from your home to one landmark using imperatives.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
"Asking for and Giving Directions in English"

A practical lesson on how to ask for directions, give directions with imperatives, and use prepositions of place. Connection to Lesson 14: exactly the language Marcos needs to get around Washington.

Tip: pause after each model and give the directions aloud yourself.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
Billions -- business dinners and social invitations

A series full of high-stakes business dinners, invitations and social networking. Direct connection to Lesson 15: notice how characters invite each other, accept and decline, and build relationships outside the meeting room.

Tip: note 3 invitations and how each one is accepted or declined ("I'd love to", "I'm afraid I can't").

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Business English -- "Making and Responding to Invitations"

Practical episodes on inviting people, accepting warmly and declining politely in professional settings. Direct connection to Lesson 15: Would you like to...? / How about...? and I'd love to / I'm afraid I can't.

Tip: write one invitation and two replies (one accepting, one declining) using the phrases from this lesson.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
"How to Make, Accept and Decline Invitations in English"

A practical lesson on inviting people, accepting warmly and saying no politely. Connection to Lesson 15: exactly the language Marcos needs to build relationships over social dinners at the congress.

Tip: pause after each model and say the invitation and reply aloud yourself.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
Succession -- ambition, goals and the future of a company

A series about leadership, ambition and long-term plans inside a powerful company. Direct connection to Lesson 16: listen for how characters describe their goals, make predictions ("will") and announce plans ("going to").

Tip: note 3 sentences about the future and decide if each is a plan (going to) or a prediction (will).

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Business English -- "Talking About Plans & the Future"

Practical episodes on describing goals, plans and predictions at work. Direct connection to Lesson 16: will vs going to and time expressions like next year and in five years.

Tip: write three sentences about your own future -- one plan (going to) and two predictions (will).

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
"WILL vs GOING TO -- Talking About the Future in English"

A clear lesson on the difference between will (predictions, decisions) and going to (plans, intentions). Connection to Lesson 16: exactly the grammar Marcos needs to talk about his goals and five-year plan.

Tip: pause after each example and say your own version aloud about your career.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
Billions -- high-stakes deals and negotiations

A series built around bargaining, leverage and closing deals. Direct connection to Lesson 17: listen for how characters make offers, push back ("I'm afraid that won't work") and reach a compromise.

Tip: write down 3 conditional offers you hear (If you..., we will...).

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Negotiate Anything -- "Reaching Agreement at the Table"

Practical episodes on proposals, concessions and finding common ground. Direct connection to Lesson 17: agreement language and the give-and-take of a deal.

Tip: note one phrase for proposing and one for pushing back, then say your own version.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
"First Conditional -- If + Present, Will + Verb"

A clear lesson on the first conditional and the common mistake of using "will" after "if". Connection to Lesson 17: exactly the grammar Marcos needs to make conditional offers at the negotiation table.

Tip: pause after each example and build your own conditional offer about a deal.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Series
Madam Secretary -- diplomacy, travel and high-level meetings

Characters move through airports, hotels, dinners and negotiations -- exactly the full arc you just reviewed. Connection to Lesson 18: spot every structure in one episode (requests, directions, invitations, future plans, deals).

Tip: in one scene, write down one example of each structure from the trip.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Travelling for Business" review series

Episodes covering airports, hotels, restaurants, small talk and deals -- the whole trip in audio. Connection to Lesson 18: hear all the key phrases in natural speech.

Tip: shadow one phrase per moment of the trip out loud, with confidence.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
"Survive a Business Trip Abroad -- English Phrases You Need"

A single video that walks through the airport, hotel, restaurant, directions and meetings. Connection to Lesson 18: a complete recap of the whole arc in one place.

Tip: pause at each stage and say your own version before the speaker continues.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 19 — Professional Emails & Following Up

Series
Suits -- lawyers living in their inboxes

The characters constantly send, forward and reply to emails under pressure. Connection to Lesson 19: notice how they follow up and chase pending answers.

Tip: write down one present perfect sentence you hear (e.g. "I have sent it").

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Writing Professional Emails"

Episodes on email openings, follow-ups, attachments and polite closings. Connection to Lesson 19: hear the exact phrases for reaching out and acknowledging.

Tip: shadow one full sentence with the present perfect out loud.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
"How to Write a Follow-up Email in English (with Present Perfect)"

A short guide to subject lines, attachments and polite follow-ups. Connection to Lesson 19: a full template for the email you just learned to write.

Tip: pause and write your own follow-up email to a real contact.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 20 — On a Call -- Telephone & Video Conferences

Series
The Office (US) -- conference calls gone wrong

The team is constantly on speakerphone, taking messages and dealing with bad connections. Connection to Lesson 20: notice how they answer, put people on hold and report what someone said.

Tip: write down one reported sentence you hear (e.g. "He said he would call back").

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Telephone English & Taking Messages"

Episodes on answering calls, taking and leaving messages, and joining video conferences. Connection to Lesson 20: hear the exact phrases for "Marcos speaking", "Can I take a message?" and reporting what someone said.

Tip: shadow one full sentence in reported speech out loud.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
"Telephone English: How to Take and Leave a Message (with Reported Speech)"

A short guide to answering, taking messages and handling video-call problems. Connection to Lesson 20: a full template for the calls you just practiced.

Tip: pause and report a real message from this week to a friend or to yourself.

Watch on YouTube ↗
